sn45.4:1.3: For Jānussoṇi, see SN 12.47:1.2 and note. | A chariot drawn by mares was the preferred vehicle of Jānussoṇi (MN 99:30.1, MN 27:2.1), as well as the brahmin student Ambaṭṭha DN 3:1.6.1. Chāndogya Upaniṣad 4.2.1 depicts Jānaśruti’s wealth as including cows, gold, and a chariot drawn by mules.
sn45.4:7.1: Verses connecting parts of the Dhamma to a chariot are also found at SN 1.46 and SN 41.5.
sn45.4:8.1: The yassa (“of which …”) with which the verses open is not answered until etad (“this”) in the final verse. This creates a suspension over the entire text, like an unresolved note in a chord sequence. The suspense is further heightened by the absence of active verbs, drawing out the moment when movement finally starts; and it is literal movement, as the chariot “rolls” right into the resolution of “this” in the final verse. Only then is the true nature of the “divine vehicle” revealed. Such elaborate literary conceits, rare in the Suttas, are common in the Vedas. It is employed here to evoke the sophistication of Vedic verse, paralleling the luxury of the chariot. While I normally do not attempt to mimic Pali syntax so literally, here I feel the poetic effect is dramatic enough that it’s worth trying.
sn45.4:8.3: It is not easy to differentiate the dhura (“shaft”) from the īsā (“draft-pole”). The īsā is like the tusks of an elephant (MN 61.7.1, Ud 6.4:24.2), a forepart that might collide (SN 11.6:2.2), and which a colt might shake off (AN 8.14:12.4). Certain ancient images, such as the Indus Valley Daimabad toy chariot, clearly show a second part, a thicker “shaft” with a cylindrical end into which the draft-pole was fitted, which is perhaps the load-bearing dhura.
sn45.4:9.1: Here at the start of the second verse is a partial resolution as the subject, left implicit in the first verse, is revealed.
sn45.4:9.3: The Chinese parallel at SA 769 is helpful: 捨三昧為轅.
sn45.4:10.3: As the soma “endures” the blows of the pressing stone (Rig Veda 2.13.3).
sn45.4:11.1: The text plays with Vedic expressions, but the intent is purely Buddhist—the path rolls onwards due to one’s own practice.
